an6.63:1.1: “I will teach you, *bhikkhus*, a penetrative exposition, an exposition of *dhamma*.
an6.63:1.2: Listen to it, attend carefully, and I will speak.”
an6.63:10.2: Contact, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:11.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:11.2: One sensual pleasure, *bhikkhus*, is in forms [*rūpesu*], another sensual pleasure is in sounds, another sensual pleasure is in odors, another sensual pleasure is in tastes, another sensual pleasure is in tangibles.
an6.63:11.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:12.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:12.2: When one desiring, *bhikkhus*, produces one or another corresponding state of existence, whether connected with merit or connected with demerit, this is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:13.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of sensual pleasures?
an6.63:13.2: The cessation of contact, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:13.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures, that is—
an6.63:13.4: right view, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right *sati*, right *samādhi*.
an6.63:14.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands sensual pleasures in this way, understands the source and origin of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the diversity of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the result of sensual pleasures in this way, understands the cessation of sensual pleasures in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of sensual pleasures.
an6.63:14.2: Sensual pleasures, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood;
an6.63:14.3: what was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:15.1: *Vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood;
an6.63:15.2: thus it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
an6.63:16.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *vedanās*?
an6.63:16.2: Contact, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *vedanās*.
an6.63:17.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of *vedanās*?
an6.63:17.2: There is, *bhikkhus*, worldly pleasant *vedanā*; there is unworldly pleasant *vedanā*; there is worldly painful *vedanā*; there is unworldly painful *vedanā*; there is worldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*; there is unworldly neither-painful-nor-pleasant *vedanā*.
an6.63:17.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of *vedanās*.
an6.63:18.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of *vedanās*?
an6.63:18.2: When one feeling, *bhikkhus*, produces one or another corresponding state of existence, whether connected with merit or connected with demerit,
an6.63:18.3: this is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of *vedanās*.
an6.63:19.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *vedanā*?
an6.63:19.2: The cessation of contact, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *vedanā*.
an6.63:19.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā*, that is—
an6.63:2.1: “And what, *bhikkhus*, is that penetrative exposition, that exposition of *dhamma*?
an6.63:2.2: Sensual pleasures, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of sensual pleasures should be understood; the diversity of sensual pleasures should be understood; the result of sensual pleasures should be understood; the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood.
an6.63:20.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands *vedanā* in this way, understands the source and origin of *vedanās* in this way, understands the diversity of *vedanās* in this way, understands the result of *vedanās* in this way, understands the cessation of *vedanā* in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of *vedanā*.
an6.63:20.2: *Vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood;
an6.63:20.3: what was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:21.1: *Saññā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood;
an6.63:21.2: thus it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
an6.63:21.4: *saññā* of form [*rūpasaññā*], *saññā* of sounds, *saññā* of odors, *saññā* of tastes, *saññā* of tangibles, and *saññā* of *dhammas*.
an6.63:22.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *saññās*?
an6.63:22.2: Contact, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *saññās*.
an6.63:23.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of *saññās*?
an6.63:23.2: One *saññā*, *bhikkhus*, is in forms [*rūpesu*], another *saññā* is in sounds, another *saññā* is in odors, another *saññā* is in tastes, another *saññā* is in tangibles, another *saññā* is in *dhammas*.
an6.63:23.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of *saññās*.
an6.63:24.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of *saññās*?
an6.63:24.2: I say, *bhikkhus*, that *saññā* has expression as its fruit.
an6.63:24.3: According as one perceives it, so one expresses it: ‘I was one who had such a *saññā*.’
an6.63:24.4: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of *saññās*.
an6.63:25.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *saññā*?
an6.63:25.2: The cessation of contact, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *saññā*.
an6.63:25.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā*, that is—
an6.63:26.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands *saññā* in this way, understands the source and origin of *saññās* in this way, understands the diversity of *saññās* in this way, understands the result of *saññās* in this way, understands the cessation of *saññā* in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of *saññā*.
an6.63:26.2: *Saññā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood.
an6.63:26.3: What was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:27.1: *Āsavas*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood;
an6.63:27.2: thus it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
an6.63:27.4: the *āsava* of sensual pleasure, the *āsava* of *bhava*, and the *āsava* of blindness [*avijjāsavo*].
an6.63:28.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *āsavas*?
an6.63:28.2: Blindness [*Avijjā*], *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *āsavas*.
an6.63:29.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of *āsavas*?
an6.63:29.2: There are, *bhikkhus*, *āsavas* leading to hell; there are *āsavas* leading to the animal womb; there are *āsavas* leading to the ghost realm; there are *āsavas* leading to the human world; there are *āsavas* leading to the world of the devas.
an6.63:29.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of *āsavas*.
an6.63:3.1: *Vedanā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *vedanās* should be understood; the diversity of *vedanās* should be understood; the result of *vedanās* should be understood; the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *vedanā* should be understood.
an6.63:30.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of *āsavas*?
an6.63:30.2: When one gone into blindness [*avijjāgato*], *bhikkhus*, produces one or another corresponding state of existence, whether connected with merit or connected with demerit, this is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of *āsavas*.
an6.63:31.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *āsavas*?
an6.63:31.2: The cessation of blindness [*Avijjānirodho*], *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *āsavas*.
an6.63:31.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas*, that is—
an6.63:32.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands *āsavas* in this way, understands the source and origin of *āsavas* in this way, understands the diversity of *āsavas* in this way, understands the result of *āsavas* in this way, understands the cessation of *āsavas* in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of *āsavas*.
an6.63:32.2: *Āsavas*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood.
an6.63:32.3: What was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:33.1: *Kamma*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood; thus it was said.
an6.63:33.2: In reference to what was this said?
an6.63:33.3: It is intention, *bhikkhus*, that I call *kamma*.
an6.63:34.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *kammas*?
an6.63:34.2: Contact, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *kammas*.
an6.63:35.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of *kammas*?
an6.63:35.2: There is, *bhikkhus*, *kamma* to be felt in hell; there is *kamma* to be felt in the animal womb; there is *kamma* to be felt in the ghost realm; there is *kamma* to be felt in the human world; there is *kamma* to be felt in the world of the devas.
an6.63:35.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of *kammas*.
an6.63:36.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of *kammas*?
an6.63:36.2: I say, *bhikkhus*, that the result of *kammas* is threefold—
an6.63:36.3: in this very life, on rebirth, or on some later occasion.
an6.63:36.4: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of *kammas*.
an6.63:37.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *kamma*?
an6.63:37.2: The cessation of contact, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *kamma*.
an6.63:37.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma*, that is—
an6.63:38.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands *kamma* in this way, understands the source and origin of *kammas* in this way, understands the diversity of *kammas* in this way, understands the result of *kammas* in this way, understands the cessation of *kamma* in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of *kamma*.
an6.63:38.2: *Kamma*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood … the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood;
an6.63:38.3: what was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:39.1: *Dukkha*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:39.2: Thus it was said. And in reference to what was this said?
an6.63:39.3: Birth is *dukkha*, aging is *dukkha*, illness is *dukkha*, death is *dukkha*; sorrow, lamentation, pain, distress, and despair are *dukkha*; not obtaining what one wishes for is *dukkha*; in brief, the five aggregates of *upādāna* are *dukkha*.
an6.63:4.1: *Saññā*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *saññās* should be understood; the diversity of *saññās* should be understood; the result of *saññās* should be understood; the cessation of *saññā* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *saññā* should be understood.
an6.63:40.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *dukkha*?
an6.63:40.2: *Taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of *dukkha*.
an6.63:41.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the diversity of *dukkha*?
an6.63:41.2: There is, *bhikkhus*, severe *dukkha*; there is slight *dukkha*; there is *dukkha* that fades slowly; there is *dukkha* that fades quickly.
an6.63:41.3: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the diversity of *dukkha*.
an6.63:42.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the result of *dukkha*?
an6.63:42.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, someone overcome by a certain *dukkha*, with mind consumed by it, sorrows, wearies, laments, beats the breast and cries, and falls into delusion; or else, overcome by a certain *dukkha*, with mind consumed by it, he undertakes a search outside:
an6.63:42.3: ‘Who knows a word or two for the cessation of this *dukkha*?’
an6.63:42.4: I say, *bhikkhus*, that *dukkha* has delusion as its fruit, or search as its fruit.
an6.63:42.5: This is called, *bhikkhus*, the result of *dukkha*.
an6.63:43.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *dukkha*?
an6.63:43.2: The cessation of *taṇhā*, *bhikkhus*, is the cessation of *dukkha*.
an6.63:43.3: This noble eightfold path is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*, that is—
an6.63:44.1: When, *bhikkhus*, a noble disciple understands *dukkha* in this way, understands the source and origin of *dukkha* in this way, understands the diversity of *dukkha* in this way, understands the result of *dukkha* in this way, understands the cessation of *dukkha* in this way, and understands the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* in this way, then he understands this penetrative spiritual life as the cessation of *dukkha*.
an6.63:44.2: *Dukkha*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:44.3: What was said thus was said in reference to this.
an6.63:45.1: This, *bhikkhus*, is that penetrative exposition, that exposition of *dhamma*.”
an6.63:5.1: *Āsavas*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *āsavas* should be understood; the diversity of *āsavas* should be understood; the result of *āsavas* should be understood; the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *āsavas* should be understood.
an6.63:6.1: *Kamma*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *kammas* should be understood; the diversity of *kammas* should be understood; the result of *kammas* should be understood; the cessation of *kamma* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *kamma* should be understood.
an6.63:7.1: *Dukkha*, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of *dukkha* should be understood; the diversity of *dukkha* should be understood; the result of *dukkha* should be understood; the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha* should be understood.
an6.63:8.1: ‘Sensual pleasures, *bhikkhus*, should be understood; the source and origin of sensual pleasures should be understood; the diversity of sensual pleasures should be understood; the result of sensual pleasures should be understood; the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood; the practice leading to the cessation of sensual pleasures should be understood,’ thus it was said.
an6.63:8.10: Passion for intentions is a person’s sensual pleasure,
an6.63:8.11: not the beautiful things in the world;
an6.63:8.12: passion for intentions is a person’s sensual pleasure,
an6.63:8.13: the beautiful things remain just as they are in the world;
an6.63:8.14: but here the wise remove desire.
an6.63:8.2: In reference to what was this said?
an6.63:8.3: There are these five strands of sensual pleasure, *bhikkhus*—
an6.63:8.4: forms [*rūpā*] known by the eye, wished for, desired, agreeable, endearing, connected with sensual pleasure, enticing;
an6.63:8.8: tangibles known by the body, wished for, desired, agreeable, endearing, connected with sensual pleasure, enticing.
an6.63:8.9: But these, *bhikkhus*, are not sensual pleasures; these are called strands of sensual pleasure in the noble one’s *vinaya*—
an6.63:9.1: And what, *bhikkhus*, is the source and origin of sensual pleasures?
